title: Graph theory: basic question on material week 6

questions.text: Consider the complete graph on 5 vertices, $K_5$. How many distinct paths of length 4 are there in this graph?
questions.explanation: A path of length 4 visits exactly 5 distinct vertices. Since it's a complete graph, any sequence of 5 distinct vertices forms a valid path. The number of ways to order 5 vertices is $5! = 120$.

questions.text: Let $T$ be a tree with exactly $n$ vertices. According to the Handshaking Lemma, what must be the sum of the degrees of all vertices in $T$?
questions.explanation: A tree on $n$ vertices has exactly $n-1$ edges. By the Handshaking Lemma, the sum of all degrees is exactly twice the number of edges, which is $2(n-1) = 2n-2$.

questions.text: The hypercube graph $Q_n$ has vertices representing all binary strings of length $n$, with edges between strings that differ in exactly one position. For which values of $n$ does $Q_n$ contain an Eulerian circuit?
questions.explanation: A graph has an Eulerian circuit if and only if it is connected and all vertices have an even degree. In $Q_n$, every vertex has a degree of exactly $n$ (you can flip any one of the $n$ bits to reach a neighbor). Therefore, $n$ must be even.

questions.text: Suppose a connected graph $G$ has 10 vertices and 12 edges. Is $G$ a tree?
choices: Yes, because the number of edges is greater than the number of vertices.
No, because a tree on 10 vertices must have exactly 9 edges.
There is not enough information to determine this.
questions.explanation: A connected graph on $n$ vertices is a tree if and only if it has exactly $n-1$ edges. Since this graph has 12 edges (which is more than 9), it must contain cycles and is therefore not a tree.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about walks, trails and paths is false?
choices: Every path is also a trail.
Every trail is also a walk.
A closed trail of length $\ge 1$ with no repeated vertices (other than start/end) is a cycle.
If there is a walk between $u$ and $v$, there is a trail between them.
questions.explanation: Path and trails are walks without repeated vertices respectively edges. There is no closed trail of length 1 or 2 since edges cannot be repeated. We proved in the lectures that a walk can be turned into a path, which is then also a trail. Thus, all statements are true.

questions.text: Let $G$ be the path graph $P_3$ with vertex set $V = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and edge set $E = \{\{1, 2\}, \{2, 3\}\}$. Which of the following is a subgraph of $G$ but NOT an induced subgraph?
choices: The graph with vertices $\{1, 3\}$ and no edges.
The graph with vertices $\{1, 2\}$ and edge $\{1, 2\}$.
The graph with vertices $\{1, 2, 3\}$ and only the edge $\{1, 2\}$.
The graph with vertices $\{2\}$ and no edges.
questions.explanation: An induced subgraph $G[V']$ must contain *all* edges from the original graph $G$ that have both endpoints in $V'$. If we select the vertex set $V' = \{1, 2, 3\}$, the induced subgraph must be the original graph itself (2 edges). A graph with these 3 vertices but only 1 edge is a subgraph, but not an induced subgraph.

questions.text: What is the diameter of the cycle graph $C_{10}$?
questions.explanation: The diameter is the maximum shortest-path distance between any two vertices in the graph. In a cycle $C_n$, the two furthest vertices are exactly opposite each other, which gives a distance of $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$. For $C_{10}$, the diameter is $10/2 = 5$.

questions.text: Which of the following graphs is NOT bipartite?
questions.explanation: Kőnig's Theorem states that a graph is bipartite if and only if it contains no odd cycles. The graph $C_{15}$ is an odd cycle (length 15), so it cannot be bipartite. Trees (like paths and stars) and even cycles are always bipartite.

questions.text: Let $G$ be a simple graph on 8 vertices. According to Dirac's Theorem, what is the minimum degree required to guarantee that $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle?
questions.explanation: Dirac's Theorem guarantees a Hamiltonian cycle if every vertex has a degree of at least $n/2$. Since $n = 8$, the minimum degree required is $8/2 = 4$.

questions.text: Let $T$ be a tree with 12 vertices. What is the minimum possible number of leaves (vertices of degree 1) in $T$?
questions.explanation: Every tree with at least 2 vertices must have at least 2 leaves (which occurs when the tree is just a single long path, like $P_{12}$). It is impossible to have a tree with only 1 leaf, as that would imply a cycle or a disconnected graph.
